title: Association between urban shrinkage and excess mortality during the COVID-19 pandemic

meta_data.Data_Name: CDC WONDER county monthly all cause mortality data
meta_data.Data_summary: County-level monthly death counts from all causes retrieved from the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention WONDER system. Used to fit a pre-pandemic baseline model per county and then compute expected deaths and excess deaths during the COVID-19 period.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: Raw monthly mortality data for each county from January 2015 to March 2023 that are used for the calculation of excess death are available from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s WONDER database https://wonder.cdc.gov/ Location: Data availability Confidence: high

meta_data.Data_Name: US Bureau of Economic Analysis county gross regional domestic product GRDP
meta_data.Data_summary: County-level gross regional domestic product values used to measure local economic change and to compute compound annual growth rates as an urban shrinkage indicator and for county typology classification.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: Raw GRDP data for each county from 2010 to 2020 that are used for the severity of urban shrinkage are available from the Bureau of Economic Analysis https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas Location: Data availability Confidence: high

meta_data.Data_Name: US Census Bureau county population estimates including age specific population
meta_data.Data_summary: County-level population counts and age-specific population estimates used to compute population growth or decline and the share of older residents used as predictors and for shrinkage classification.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: Raw population data for each county including age-specific population from 2010 to 2020 are available from the US Census Bureau https://www.census.gov/data/datasets/time-series/demo/popest/2020s-counties-total.html Location: Data availability Confidence: high

meta_data.Data_Name: USDA Economic Research Service county level data sets income education unemployment
meta_data.Data_summary: County-level socioeconomic indicator tables including median household income, education attainment and unemployment rate used as explanatory variables in the mixed-effects models and descriptive comparisons across county types.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: Raw median household income, education and unemployment data for each county are available from the US Department of Agriculture https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/county-level-data-sets/ Location: Data availability Confidence: high

meta_data.Data_Name: US Census Bureau ACS 5 year table B02001 race by county
meta_data.Data_summary: County-level racial composition counts or shares from the American Community Survey table B02001 used to construct race percentage covariates for regression models and county profiling.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: Raw population ratio by race for each county is available from the US Census Bureau https://data.census.gov/table/ACSDT5Y2020.B02001 Location: Data availability Confidence: high

meta_data.Data_Name: US Census Bureau county land area geography information
meta_data.Data_summary: County area measurements used to compute population density as a control variable in statistical models and descriptive analysis.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: Raw area data for each county, required for calculating population density, are available from the US Census Bureau https://data.census.gov/table?d=GEO+Geography+Information Location: Data availability Confidence: high

meta_data.Data_Name: US Census Bureau county boundary vector data
meta_data.Data_summary: Public domain vector boundary data for US counties used to map and visualize the spatial distribution of county typologies in QGIS.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: The county boundaries were based on public domain vector data from the United States Census Bureau https://www.census.gov/en.html Location: Extended data Confidence: high

meta_data.Data_Name: Paper-level data deposit from Data availability GitHub
meta_data.Data_summary: Authors compiled county-level analytic dataset containing monthly observed, expected and excess deaths and derived shrinkage and socioeconomic variables used in modeling and descriptive analysis.
meta_data.Other_Information: Evidence: Overall, the organized data, including each county’s monthly observed, expected and excess deaths, and the CAGR of GRDP, CAGR of population, median income, education, unemployment, ratio of older people and population by race, are available via GitHub at https://github.com/GukhwaJang/journal_shrinking_covid Location: Data availability Confidence: high
